definition: Mystic describes the hidden spiritual truth open only to inner experience — and names one who seeks it. The word is about closed eyes and sealed lips.
history: Greek *mystikos*, from *mystēs*, 'an initiate,' from *myein*, 'to shut (the eyes or lips)' — a mystic is one who has closed to the outer world and sworn silence at the mysteries (the same root as *mystery* and *mute*). Christian, Sufi, and Kabbalist mysticisms all press toward a union beyond words. Magic's Mystics skew blue, the color of hidden knowledge.
commentary: 'Mysticism' looks timeless but is largely a modern coinage. Where older writers spoke of contemplation, union, or the *unio mystica* as specific practices, the nineteenth and twentieth centuries assembled a single universal category — 'mystical experience,' ineffable and cross-cultural — and read it back onto Eckhart, the Sufis, and the Kabbalists alike. Leigh Eric Schmidt has traced how liberal Protestant and Romantic writers manufactured that abstraction, and Michel de Certeau, in *The Mystic Fable*, showed 'the mystics' emerging as a distinct early-modern formation rather than a perennial one. The word names something real; the *-ism* names a scholarly habit.

commentary_sources.label: Leigh Eric Schmidt, 'The Making of Modern Mysticism' (JAAR, 2003)
commentary_sources.abstract: This journal article argues that today's popular idea of 'mysticism' — an intensely personal, direct spiritual experience separate from organized religion — is not an ancient concept but a fairly modern invention. Schmidt traces how it first took shape in English Enlightenment-era religious arguments and was then reshaped again by 19th-century American Transcendentalist thinkers.

commentary_sources.label: Michel de Certeau, The Mystic Fable
commentary_sources.abstract: This book examines Christian mysticism — the belief in a direct, personal encounter with God — as it appeared in Europe during the 1500s and 1600s. De Certeau treats mystical writing as a distinctive kind of language that emerged at a moment when older, shared religious certainties were starting to break down.

commentary_sources.label: Steven Katz, Mysticism and Philosophical Analysis
commentary_sources.abstract: This edited collection of essays argues that mystical experiences (intense, direct feelings of contact with the divine or ultimate reality) are shaped by the beliefs and culture of the person having them, meaning a Christian mystic and a Buddhist mystic do not actually have the identical experience described in different words.

etymology.origin: Greek mystikos, from mystēs ‘an initiate’, from myein ‘to shut (the eyes/lips)’
etymology.note: A mystic is one who has ‘shut their eyes’ — closed to the outer world, sworn to secrecy at the mysteries. Same root as ‘mystery’ and ‘mute.’

flavor_snippets.html:
"Raze the body. Raise the soul."
—<mark>Mystic</mark> elder

"It's endearing, in a <mark>mystical</mark>, pointy sort of way."
—General Tazri, allied commander
The <mark>mystic</mark> bond between spellcasters and the land is just as vulnerable to attack as the casters themselves.
The curse of Redtooth Keep could only be broken by a <mark>mystical</mark> rose that blooms in moonlight.
Rimewind necromancers caught travelers in <mark>mystical</mark> blizzards then trapped their spirits in the mist of their final frozen breaths.

"Can you catch a snowflake on the edge of your blade? Her sword has snagged an entire blizzard."
—Zogye, Ojutai <mark>mystic</mark>
Cinder <mark>mystics</mark> set traps for sluaghs and other spirits of flame, seeking to extract the secrets of the fire their own hearts have lost.
The Hand is comprised of many ninja factions united under the banner of the Beast, an ancient demon who grants them dark, <mark>mystical</mark> strength.

"Your corruption will burn, serpent, until there is nothing left to defile."
—Asmala, bloodfire <mark>mystic</mark>

"The animal spirits are the Ancestor's grandchildren. Remember them."
—<mark>Mystic</mark> elder

"The hum of the universe is never off-key."
—<mark>Mystic</mark> elder

"Be as water: untamed and unheld, yet inexorably flowing toward a greater goal."
—Shensu, Riverwheel <mark>mystic</mark>
The Seekers of Carmot searched across the unknown lands for the <mark>mystical</mark> red stone that could reforge Esper in ethereal perfection.
Some emeritus professors return from their wanderings periodically to share tales of the <mark>mystical</mark> wonders of Arcavios.

mechanics_snippets.html: Choose a card at random from among Righteous Confluence, <mark>Mystic</mark> Confluence, Wretched Confluence, Fiery Confluence, Verdant Confluence, Brokers Confluence, Cabaretti Confluence, Maestros Confluence, Obscura Confluence, and Riveteers Confluence. Create a copy of that card. You may cast the copy without paying its mana cost. (Roll a D10 to determine the random card.)
As this land enters, draft a card from The <mark>Mystical</mark> Archive's spellbook and exile it face down.

As an additional cost to play Banned Eldraine Card, pay {B}, {3}{R}{G}, {3}{R}, {2}, {0}, or {1}{G}. It becomes a copy respectively of Cauldron Familiar, Escape to the Wilds, Fires of Invention, Lucky Clover, <mark>Mystic</mark> Sanctuary, Once Upon A Time for the rest of the game in all zones. (You can't choose Oko. Because let's be real, nobody wants to play against Oko again.)

Deathtouch
<mark>Mystic</mark> Arcanum — At the beginning of your end step, exile the top card of your library. Until the end of your next turn, you may play that card.
Pact Boon — Whenever you play a card from exile, create a Treasure token.

Flying, haste
Whenever you cast an instant, sorcery, or Wizard spell, Umara <mark>Mystic</mark> gets +2/+0 until end of turn.

−3: Choose a card name from among Enlightened Tutor, <mark>Mystical</mark> Tutor, Booster Tutor, Imperial Recruiter, and Worldly Tutor. Create a copy of the card with the chosen name. You may cast the copy without paying its mana cost.
